description: The provided image is an invalid placeholder displaying a 'Check for updates' icon, which has no correlation with the research content of the paper focusing on strengthening mechanisms in Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels. No material characterization results, microstructural observations, property test data, or other academic information relevant to the paper's research scope can be identified from this placeholder image, so no valid scientific conclusions related to the Fe-Al alloy system can be derived from it.

This figure presents Mössbauer spectra (left column, transmission (%) vs. velocity (mm/s)) and corresponding hyperfine field (B_hf) probability distributions (right column, P(B_hf) vs. B_hf (T)) for five samples: (a,b) α-Fe foil reference, (c,d) Fe-5Al, (e,f) Fe-6Al, (g,h) Fe-6.8Al, and (i,j) Fe-9Al ferritic low-density steels. Red dots represent experimental data, black lines are fitting curves matching the experimental data, and green curves on the right are deconvoluted P(B_hf) components corresponding to Fe atoms with different numbers of Al nearest neighbors. As Al content increases, the single dominant P(B_hf) peak (~33 T) of pure Fe gradually splits into multiple components with overall B_hf shifting to lower values, indicating Al incorporation into the Fe lattice modifies the local magnetic environment of Fe atoms. These results provide direct spectroscopic evidence for the existence of DO3-type Fe3Al ordered nanophases in the Fe-Al alloy system, supporting ordered phase strengthening contribution calculations in subsequent strengthening mechanism analysis.

This figure presents four 2D small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) profiles of Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels, where subplot (a) corresponds to Fe-5Al, (b) to Fe-6Al, (c) to Fe-6.8Al, and (d) to Fe-9Al alloy. All tested powder samples were pre-annealed at 600 °C under vacuum for 60 min to eliminate internal stress before SAXS measurement. All four subplots exhibit centrally symmetric annular scattering patterns, verifying the uniform dispersion of nanoscale DO3-type Fe3Al ordered precipitates in the BCC ferrite matrix. As Al content rises from 5 wt% to 9 wt%, the scattering features match the trend that the volume fraction of ordered precipitates increases from 0.4% to 1.9%, and their average size increases from 2.5 nm to 3.75 nm. These results provide direct experimental evidence for the composition-dependent evolution of nano-ordered phases, supporting the subsequent strengthening mechanism analysis for the studied low-density steels.

This figure displays the variation of logarithmic small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) intensity as a function of scattering vector q (unit: nm⁻¹) for four Fe-Al low-density steel samples with different Al contents: Fe-5Al, Fe-6Al, Fe-6.8Al, and Fe-9Al. Red open symbols (circle, square, triangle, diamond corresponding to the four alloys respectively) represent experimental SAXS data, while dark blue solid lines are fitted curves using Eq. (3) reported in the work. All curves are vertically offset to avoid overlapping. The SAXS intensity of all samples decreases with increasing q, and the low-q region matches the q⁻⁴ scaling relation noted in the caption. This SAXS data is used to quantitatively characterize the size, volume fraction and number density of nano-sized DO3 Fe3Al ordered precipitates in Fe-Al alloys, verifying the presence of uniformly dispersed ordered nanoprecipitates in all samples, and providing experimental evidence for subsequent strengthening mechanism analysis of these low-density steels.

This chart plots the variation of two microstructural parameters of nano-sized DO3 Fe3Al ordered phase in Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels as a function of Al weight percentage (Wt% Al, x-axis). The left y-axis shows volume fraction of the ordered phase (unit: %, represented by black circles), while the right y-axis shows the diameter of the ordered phase (unit: nm, represented by black squares). As Al content increases from 5 wt% to 9 wt%, the volume fraction of Fe3Al phase increases almost linearly from ~0.4% to ~1.95%, and the phase diameter first fluctuates between ~2.6 nm and ~2.8 nm at 5-7 wt% Al, then rises significantly to ~3.6 nm at 9 wt% Al, with error bars indicating measurement uncertainty. The data are derived from Small Angle X-Ray Scattering (SAXS) characterization, which show good consistency with TEM measurement results of ordered phase size. This figure confirms the existence of nano-sized Fe3Al ordered precipitates in the studied alloys, and supports the conclusion that these ordered phases act as an additional strengthening contributor to the ferritic low-density steels.

This is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of Fe-6.8 wt% Al ferritic low-density steel sample, which was subjected to 98% cold rolling prior to isothermal annealing at 750 °C for 60 min, with a 300 μm scale bar marked at the bottom right. The microstructure consists of fully recrystallized, uniform equiaxed single-phase BCC ferrite grains without residual deformed microstructures, and the average grain size is confirmed to be 48.3 ± 1.2 μm. Obvious grain growth is observed compared with the sample annealed at the same temperature for 5 min (average grain size 25.2 ± 5.0 μm). This figure confirms that prolonged annealing duration after cold deformation leads to grain coarsening in the alloy, which reduces the grain boundary strengthening contribution, thus resulting in decreased yield strength and increased ductility as verified in mechanical tests, supporting the analysis of strengthening mechanism variations during post-deformation annealing processing.

This figure presents a dark-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph of Fe-6.8 wt% Al ferritic low-density steel, with a 200 nm scale bar at the bottom right. Bright fine particles marked by red arrows are nanoscale DO3-type Fe3Al ordered phases uniformly dispersed in the BCC ferrite matrix. The inset shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern collected from the [011] zone axis of the Fe-Al matrix: strong diffraction spots correspond to matrix lattice reflections (labeled as $\overline{2}00$, $\overline{2}11$, $01\overline{1}$ planes), while weak superlattice spots from the ordered phase are highlighted by yellow arrows. This figure directly confirms the existence of uniformly distributed nano-sized ordered precipitates in the Fe-6.8Al alloy, provides direct morphological and structural evidence for ordered phase strengthening contribution, and shows good consistency with complementary characterization results from SAXS and Mössbauer spectroscopy.

This figure focuses on Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel under three processing conditions: 98% cold rolled (98%CR), isothermally annealed at 750 °C for 5 min, and annealed at 750 °C for 60 min. Subplot (a) shows the normalized (110) XRD peak profiles, with inset presenting full XRD patterns confirming single BCC ferrite phase for all samples; the 98%CR sample has the broadest peak, which becomes sharper with increasing annealing time, indicating reduced lattice microstrain. Subplots (b), (c), (d) plot the second order moment M2 and fourth order moment M4/q² as functions of q for the three samples respectively, used for dislocation density calculation. Subplot (e) shows that dislocation density drops by 3 orders of magnitude from ~2.2×10^15 m⁻² for 98%CR sample to ~1×10^12 m⁻² after 60 min annealing, attributed to dislocation annihilation during recrystallization. The results support that recrystallization-induced dislocation density reduction is a key reason for strength decrease after annealing of cold rolled Fe-6.8Al steel.

This figure includes two subplots presenting mechanical performance of Fe-6.8Al ferritic low-density steel processed under three conditions: 98% cold rolling (CR), 750 °C isothermal annealing for 5 min, and 750 °C isothermal annealing for 60 min. The first subplot shows engineering stress-strain curves: the 98% CR sample achieves the highest peak engineering stress of ~1800 MPa but has very low failure engineering strain below 5%; the 5 min annealed sample has an ultimate tensile strength of ~870 MPa and failure strain of ~30%; the 60 min annealed sample shows reduced strength (~390 MPa ultimate tensile strength) but higher failure strain of ~38%. The second subplot displays strain hardening rate against true strain for the two annealed samples: the 60 min annealed sample has consistently higher strain hardening rate than the 5 min annealed sample, both curves decline sharply at small true strain, undergo a small rise, then decrease continuously to near zero at ~0.2 true strain. These results indicate that extended annealing reduces strength while improving ductility of the cold-rolled steel, and grain growth during prolonged annealing leads to higher strain hardening capacity.

This stacked bar chart quantifies the contribution of five strengthening mechanisms to the yield strength (YS) of Fe-6.8wt%Al ferritic low-density steel under three processing conditions: 98% cold rolling (CR, YS=1800MPa), isothermal annealing at 750℃ for 5min (YS=700MPa), and isothermal annealing at 750℃ for 60min (YS=265MPa). The five strengthening contributions are ordering (green), grain boundary strengthening (blue), solid solution strengthening (black), dislocation strengthening (magenta), and lattice frictional stress (red). Dislocation and grain boundary strengthening dominate the high strength of the cold-rolled sample, while their contributions drop significantly after annealing due to reduced dislocation density and grain growth. Overall YS decreases with longer annealing time, and the contribution of ordering and solid solution strengthening becomes relatively more prominent in annealed samples, validating the varying dominant strengthening mechanisms under different thermomechanical processing routes for Fe-Al low-density steels.
